basic_info.name: Membrane Separation Selectivity
basic_info.description: HSP distance differences between a target permeate, an impurity (or rejected species), and the membrane material are used to predict separation selectivity factors in liquid-phase membrane processes. By calculating Ra values for target-membrane and impurity-membrane pairs, the relative affinity of each species for the membrane matrix can be quantified. A membrane material with low Ra to the target permeate and high Ra to the impurity will preferentially dissolve and transport the target, yielding high separation factors. This approach is applied in pervaporation, organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN), and liquid membrane processes for applications including solvent dehydration, organic-organic separation, and wastewater treatment.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Membrane separation selectivity in solution-diffusion membranes depends on differential solubility and diffusivity of components in the membrane material. HSP distance Ra between each permeating species and the membrane polymer quantifies thermodynamic affinity: smaller Ra means higher solubility in the membrane phase. The selectivity factor alpha is approximated by the ratio of solubility coefficients, which correlate inversely with Ra values. For pervaporation and OSN, the solubility selectivity component often dominates, making HSP-based screening particularly effective. The difference Delta_Ra = Ra(impurity-membrane) - Ra(target-membrane) serves as a selectivity predictor: larger positive Delta_Ra indicates better separation. For swelling-controlled transport, RED values help identify membranes that resist excessive swelling by the feed mixture while maintaining adequate permeability for the target.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra_i = sqrt(4*(dD_i - dD_membrane)^2 + (dP_i - dP_membrane)^2 + (dH_i - dH_membrane)^2); Selectivity predictor: Delta_Ra = Ra_impurity - Ra_target; Separation factor alpha ~ exp((Ra_impurity^2 - Ra_target^2) * V_m / (R * T))
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED(target-membrane) < 1.0 for adequate permeability; RED(impurity-membrane) > 1.2 for good rejection; Delta_Ra > 3 MPa^0.5 for meaningful selectivity

required_inputs: HSP of target permeate (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
HSP of impurity/rejected species (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
HSP and interaction radius R0 of membrane polymer
data_sources: HSPiP database for common solvents and polymers
Group contribution methods for novel polymers (Van Krevelen, Hoftyzer)
Experimental swelling tests to determine membrane HSP and R0
Literature compilations of organic solvent HSP
complementary_parameters: Molecular size and shape of permeants (for diffusivity selectivity)

accuracy.limitations: HSP-based selectivity prediction primarily captures solubility selectivity and does not account for diffusivity selectivity, which depends on molecular size, shape, and membrane free volume distribution. For rigid glassy polymer membranes, size-sieving can dominate selectivity, reducing HSP predictive accuracy. Membrane swelling by feed mixtures changes the polymer matrix HSP in situ, creating a coupling effect not captured by static HSP values. Crosslinked membranes have modified HSP compared to their linear precursors, and crosslink density effects are not directly modeled. Multi-component feed interactions (coupling effects, competitive sorption) can cause deviations from binary HSP predictions. Concentration polarization and boundary layer effects at the membrane surface are not addressed by thermodynamic HSP analysis.
known_failure_modes: Size-sieving dominated separations in tight nanofiltration membranes where diffusivity selectivity overrides solubility selectivity
Highly swollen membranes where in-situ HSP deviate significantly from dry membrane values
Azeotropic or close-boiling mixtures where both components have similar HSP to the membrane
Mixed feed effects with strong non-ideal solution behavior in the membrane phase
Ceramic or inorganic membranes where surface adsorption rather than bulk dissolution controls selectivity
accuracy.validation_method: Pervaporation or nanofiltration experiments measuring flux and separation factor for target/impurity mixtures, sorption-desorption experiments for individual component uptake, swelling degree measurements in pure and mixed solvents

key_references.author: Mulder, M.H.V.; Smolders, C.A.
key_references.title: On the mechanism of separation of ethanol/water mixtures by pervaporation

key_references.author: Jonquières, A.; Clément, R.; Lochon, P.; Néel, J.; Dresch, M.; Chrétien, B.
key_references.title: Industrial state-of-the-art of pervaporation and vapour permeation in the western countries

key_references.title: Solubility parameters and polymer membrane selection for pervaporation

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

literature.regulatory_relevance: No direct regulatory mandate for HSP-based membrane selection, but pervaporation membranes for solvent dehydration in pharmaceutical manufacturing must comply with GMP requirements. HSP screening supports solvent selection under REACH and ICH Q3C guidelines for residual solvent control. EU and EPA water treatment regulations drive membrane development where HSP screening aids material selection.

commercial_tools: Aspen Plus (process simulation with membrane modules)
MATLAB/Python custom scripts for multi-component HSP optimization
